request.question: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?

title: NFL Street 2
Braylon Edwards
Johnnie Morant
NFL Tour
Bumpin' My Music
NFL Street 3
J. D. Hill
Chad Johnson
NFL Street
Kenny Stafford
sentences: NFL Street 2 is American football video game developed by EA Sports BIG and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on December 22, 2004.
It features then-New York Giants tight end Jeremy Shockey and rapper Xzibit on the cover.
The game is compatible with "Madden NFL 2006", as players can import their "Own The City" players to it.
The game's compatibility also extends to other EA games like "", "SSX 3", "Madden NFL 2005", "NCAA Football 2005", "NFL Street", "", "NBA Live 2005", and "NBA Street Vol.
2", where if players have these games on their memory cards, they can earn 25,000 points in the game.
Braylon Jamel Edwards (born February 21, 1983) is a former American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football for the University of Michigan, earned unanimous All-American honors, and was recognized as the top college wide receiver.
He was also the first receiver in Big Ten Conference history to record three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and only the third to do so in NCAA Division I-A.
He was selected by the Cleveland Browns with the third overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.
Johnnie Morant, Jr. (born December 7, 1981) is a former gridiron football wide receiver.
He was drafted by the Oakland Raiders in the fifth round of the 2004 NFL Draft.
He played college football at Syracuse.
He is currently the Head Strength and Conditioning Coach and Wide Receivers coach at East Carter High School.
While at Kentucky Christian University, Coach Morant help mentor an All-American Wide Receiver who was invited to an All Star game and an All Mid-South Academic Wide Receiver.
NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.
It was released on January 8, 2008.
It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.
The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).
Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.
"Bumpin' My Music" is a 2006 single by Ray Cash as his third single from "C.O.D. (Cash on Delivery)".
This song is Cash's first single to reach any chart in "Billboard" magazine or to be featured on a countdown.
/ /chart .
The track is the third song on the album with a running time of 4:01.
Scarface, who is an idol of Ray Cash is featured on the song.
For a period of time, it was featured on BET 106 & Park voting page.
It was also featured on the video game NFL Street 3.
NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.
This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.
Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

J. D. Hill (born October 30, 1948, in Stockton, California) is a retired American football wide receiver who played seven seasons in the National Football League for the Buffalo Bills and Detroit Lions; he played college football at Arizona State University.
His son, Lonzell Hill, also played wide receiver in the NFL.
Another son, Shelby Hill, was a wide receiver for Syracuse University.
Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.

He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.
He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.
In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.
In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.

He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.
NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.
Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.
The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
Kenny Stafford (born April 20, 1990) is a Canadian football wide receiver who is currently a member of the Edmonton Eskimos of the Canadian Football League (CFL).
In the United States he has been a member of the Atlanta Falcons (NFL), Pittsburgh Power (AFL) and Miami Dolphins (NFL).
Elsewhere in the CFL Stafford has been a member of the Calgary Stampeders, Montreal Alouettes and Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
He is the nephew of former American football wide receiver and 2013 Hall of Fame Inductee Cris Carter.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?
hop1_docs: NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

Tai Streets | Tai Lamar Streets (born April 20, 1977) is a former professional American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).  He was selected with the second pick of the sixth round of the 1999 NFL Draft by the San Francisco 49ers.  He also played for the Detroit Lions in 2004.  He was the leading receiver for the national champion 1997 Michigan Wolverines football team.  Over the course of his career he was notable for fourth quarter performances in various bowl games and NFL playoff games.  As a professional athlete, he was known for his modesty.  As an amateur athlete, he was known as one of the best high school athletes in the city of Chicago.

NFL Street (series) | NFL Street is a series of sports video games developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It combines the talent and big names of the NFL with the atmosphere of street football.  It is much like the regular Madden NFL series, however plays are, in fact different.

Devin Street | Devin Curtis Street (born March 30, 1991) is an American football wide receiver who is currently a free agent.  He is in the National Football League (NFL).  He was drafted by the Dallas Cowboys in the fifth round of the 2014 NFL Draft.  He played college football at the University of Pittsburgh.

ESPN NFL 2K5 | ESPN NFL 2K5 is an American football video game developed by Visual Concepts and published by 2K Sports and the Sega Corporation.  It is the sixth installment of the "NFL 2K series".  The game was originally released on July 20, 2004, for both the PlayStation 2 and Xbox video game consoles.  It was the last "NFL 2K" game to be released before Electronic Arts signed an exclusivity deal with the NFL to make 2K's rival Madden NFL series the only officially licensed NFL game.  It was also the last game to be made by Sega before that happened.

NFL Tour | NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.  It was released on January 8, 2008.  It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.  The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).  Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.

NFL Street | NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.  Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.  The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [1] quoted: "NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the 'NFL Street' series" (confirms game identity).
  - [1] quoted: "Chad Johnson ... was the official spokesperson of the game." (states the game's spokesperson).
  - [1] paraphrase: Chad Johnson "of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover." (links spokesperson to that player/image).
  - [2] paraphrase: Tai Streets is a former NFL wide receiver (passage contains other wide receiver entries but not tied to NFL Street 3).
  - [4] paraphrase: Devin Street is an American football wide receiver (another wide receiver entry not tied to NFL Street 3).

- Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
  - Bridge entity: Chad Johnson (mentioned in [1]) — need confirmation that this Chad Johnson is the American football wide receiver referenced by the question.
  - Missing fact: None of the supplied passages explicitly state Chad Johnson's playing position (i.e., that he is an "American football wide receiver") or his common alternate name (e.g., Chad Ochocinco). Because that fact is not present, a final answer cannot be issued from these passages alone.

- Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve missing fact:
  1. Fetch the Wikipedia page "Chad Johnson" (and/or "Chad Ochocinco") — extract the lead sentence or infobox fields: playing position (should state "wide receiver"), primary team(s) (e.g., Cincinnati Bengals), and known aliases. This will confirm the spokesperson's position.
  2. (Lower priority) Re-fetch the full "NFL Street 3" page/section "Cover/art" or "Reception" — extract the line stating the official spokesperson or cover athlete to corroborate [1].
  3. If multiple "Chad Johnson" pages appear, fetch "Chad Johnson (disambiguation)" and the candidate pages to ensure the correct individual (extract occupation/position lines).

- Conclusion: Do not produce a final answer here — confirm Chad Johnson's position via target (1) before answering.
response.hop2_query: Chad Johnson Chad Ochocinco Wikipedia lead playing position
hop2_docs: Chad Johnson (disambiguation) | Chad Johnson (born 1978; formerly Chad Ochocinco) is an American football wide receiver.

Chad Johnson | Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.  He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.  He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.  In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.  In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.  He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.

Ochocinco: The Ultimate Catch | Ochocinco: The Ultimate Catch is an American reality television series airing on VH1 starring wide receiver Chad Ochocinco.  The show places him with 85 women to choose from to find love.  In the first episode of the show, he eliminated 69 girls.  Rubi Pazmino was the winner of the show.  Fellow NFL Wide Receivers, and Ochocinco's close friends, Bernard "B-Twice" Berrian and Terrell Owens appeared on some episodes offering tips and helping Ochocinco select the girls.
Chad Johnson (ice hockey) | Chad Terry Johnson (born June 10, 1986) is a Canadian professional ice hockey goaltender currently playing for the Buffalo Sabres of the National Hockey League (NHL).
Chad Johnson (actor) | Chad Johnson is an American actor in gay pornography.

Chad Alan | Chad Alan (also known as Chad Monticue) is a musician, known for providing lead vocals for the band Pensive, and also for playing bass guitar and providing backing vocals for the bands The Juliana Theory Derek White and the Monophobics.  Currently he is a singer/songwriter for the group Vesta Chad plays bass/back up vocals for the band 1,2,3.

Chad Mock | Chad Mock (born March 23, 1984) is a professional football player who played for the BC Lions of the Canadian Football League in 2007.  He is currently a member of their practice squad.  Chad played the wide receiver position with the Lions, and wore jersey number #83.
response.summary_2: Chad Johnson was the official spokesperson of NFL Street 3. Chad Johnson (formerly Chad Ochocinco) is a former American football wide receiver who played eleven seasons in the NFL, primarily for the Cincinnati Bengals.
